dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Hemorrhage/Laceration at 234 North First Street, [STREET_ADDRESS].
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having pretty heavy vaginal bleeding and a lot of pain in my lower abdomen.
Can you tell me your name and the date today?
Yes, I'm Emily and it's January 14th.
Where are we, ma'am?
I think it's [CITY]. I live at 234 North First Street.
What made you call 911 today?
I was bleeding from down there and I thought it was heavy. I started feeling very weak too.
Can you hold your foot up on command?
Yes, here.
That was very steady. I don't see any issues with strength.
Your airway is patent, you're speaking in full sentences and not showing signs of compromise.
Respiratory rate is 20, non-labored. Let me place the pulse oximeter on your finger.
Your oxygen saturation is 100%.
Does breathing hurt?
Only when I move, but my most comfortable position is slightly upright.
I'll take your radial pulse now.
Pulse is 91, strong and regular.
Let's take your blood pressure.
Blood pressure is 99 over 58, and skin is warm and dry.
How long has the bleeding been going on?
About three weeks, and it got worse last night with large clots passing.
Did you see the hospital about it?
Yes, they told me I was having a miscarriage and I was two months along.
Have you taken any medications or have known allergies?
No known allergies, no medications-just vitamins sometimes.
Last, what led you to call 911 today?
The bleeding and pain were getting worse. I passed big clots and felt lightheaded.
Any falls or trauma involving the abdomen recently?
No, it just came with the pain of the pregnancy.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is the pain now?
A 6, but it was higher when the clots passed.
Does the pain improve with rest or worsen with movement?
It's better when I lay still and worse when I move or pass anything.
Any pain that travels or radiates elsewhere?
I have back pain, like someone punched me and it's still there.
Let's see the bathroom to assess the situation.
There's about 500 mL of blood on the floor with several large clots. No fecal or rectal discharge seen. Range of motion in your limbs is intact.
Let me take a second blood pressure reading.
Blood pressure reading is 99 over 58.
Your breathing remains 20 per minute, non-labored and even.
We've placed you on 4-lead ECG. Readings show sinus rhythm.
I'm placing the glucometer now. Result is 111 milligrams per deciliter.
I'm going to place an IV line in your arm now.
That's fine with me.
IV successfully placed at 18G. I'll attach a saline lock.
We'll slowly administer 250 mL of normal saline through the IV to help support your volume.
The first 100 mL of normal saline has been given and the dizziness you had earlier is clearing.
Given her condition and per protocol 6-2, we're treating this as a spontaneous abortion and vaginal bleeding.
Your pulse is now 84-still strong and regular.
Blood pressure is 89 over 65. Still within acceptable range.
Oxygen level remains 100%.
Breathing remains normal and we haven't seen any signs of arrhythmia.
We'll move you to the stretcher now-can you walk with us?
Yes, I can walk with steady gait.
You're steady and displaying good range of motion in all limbs. You're alert and oriented x4.
I'll make sure you're covered with a blanket and placed on five-point restraints to keep it safe.
What's your preferred hospital, ma'am?
I prefer [COMPANY_NAME].
All set, your tone is stable and you're safe with us.
I'll ensure the hospital knows we're coming with you. They've been informed of your condition.
Pulse is down to 81 now with good rhythm.
Blood pressure improved to 98 over 58.
We've reached the hospital and are preparing the handoff now.
Patient is awake and responsive-GCS of 15, AAO x4. She is G4P4A0 with a history of miscarriage.
Currently on a cardiac monitor showing sinus rhythm and no arrhythmias to report.
She was assessed as alert and oriented, with range of motion intact and clear and equal bilateral breath sounds prior to any interventions.
IV placed at 18G with normal saline administered-no complications reported.
Vital signs prior to arrival: P-81, BP-98/58, SpO₂ 100%. No glucose reading available.
Chief complaint was vaginal bleeding with associated back pain. Patient is a 29-year-old woman with no known drug allergies or medications.
Patient independently moved to the bed upon arrival and remains stable.
All field assessments and interventions have been completed and will be handed over for transport to the ED.
Oxygen saturation was briefly read as zero in later monitoring but is otherwise stable and 100% now. No known cause at this point.
The ECG monitoring remained consistent with sinus rhythm until it faltered just before arrival-either a technical issue or sensor slippage.
No further medication was administered after the initial fluid bolus and no known complications to transfer.
Blood loss visually estimated at 500 mL with clots, consistent with ongoing miscarriage.
Patient independently moved from stretcher to bed upon arrival. All systems stable at that time.
I'll run a final EKG now just to check for any new arrhythmias.
EKG shows some erratic signals again, but it's likely a sensor issue-paper doesn't show any new patterns.
I will provide a report to the ED nurse in detail. They've also been alerted of the patient's GCS and G4P4A0 status.
Bed rails raised, staying upright with support. Traced and verified fluids were administered. All interventions per protocol 6-2.
If there's any change in GCS or other symptoms while en route, they'll be your immediate priority.
No new chest pain or breathing difficulty reported during transport.
I noticed the skin remains warm and dry throughout the transport with good color.
No rash, bruising, or signs of poor perfusion detected from head to toe.
We'll monitor any changes to color, temperature, or dryness if needed during transit.
SpO2 was briefly unregistered at last read but resumes at 100% now-likely a device issue.
Equal bilateral breath sounds with no wheeze or crackles present.
IV remains secure with no need for second-line venous access at this time.
Patient continues to tolerate IV access and fluids well.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been directly informed by radio that the patient will be incoming soon with all pending information.
